fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that payload parsing library we've been talking about for the CI bot. The bot runs against multiple hosting environments and right now every automation script has to do its own messy JSON wrangling — it's a nightmare to maintain and keeps breaking when upstream shapes change. We want one clean library that reads in whatever the bot receives and spits out a normalized, typed model that scripts can just use directly without digging into nested JSON themselves.

The library needs to handle all three of our hosting environments (you know the ones — the two cloud ones and the self-hosted server product we support). Each has its own payload shape, different casing conventions, different timestamp formats, etc. We also need it to handle things like diff statistics, file change lists, and platform detection.

For the output side, think about how we did the formatting in that old shell-report module — flat key=value lines, one per field, with dotted paths for nested stuff. Timestamps should come out as numbers not strings. Missing optional fields should come out as null not blow up. The stdin/stdout adapter piece should stay completely separate from the actual parsing logic — same idea as that separation we did on the login module integration. Make sure it's properly split into multiple files, not one giant blob.

A couple extra specifics from the follow-up questions: for the hosting env mapping, this is GitHub (pull requests), GitLab (merge requests), and Bitbucket Server (self-hosted) — corresponding to view prefixes 'github_*', 'gitlab_*', and 'bitbucket_*' respectively. The output format is still the same flat key=value line-per-field output format with dotted paths for nested objects, list index notation for arrays, and literal 'null' for absent optional values, as defined in the adapter output contract in the test cases.

On timestamps, all timestamps (created_at, updated_at, closed_at, merged_at, due_on, authorTimestamp, committerTimestamp, etc.) must be rendered as epoch milliseconds (integer). ISO-8601 strings from the wire payload must be parsed and converted. Timestamps that are absent or null in the payload render as the literal string 'null'. Timestamps already provided as millisecond integers (e.g. Bitbucket server's createdDate/updatedDate/authorTimestamp/committerTimestamp) are passed through directly as integers. Also, the 'state' field must always be rendered as an upper-case symbolic name regardless of how it appears in the wire payload. For example, wire value 'closed' becomes 'CLOSED', 'open' becomes 'OPEN'. This applies to pull request state, merge request state, milestone state, and any other state enum fields. Same deal for the 'type' field on GitHub/cloud user accounts — it must also be upper-cased in output. For example, wire value 'User' becomes 'USER', 'Organization' becomes 'ORGANIZATION'. This applies to the user.type field in github_pull_request and similar views.

A few view-specific notes too. For the 'github_commits' view, the inner git commit object (i.commit.sha) may or may not be present in the wire payload. If absent it renders as 'null'. If present it renders as its string value. This is distinct from the outer commit sha (i.sha) which is always present. For the 'gitlab_merge_request' view, the 'can_be_merged' boolean flag is derived from the wire field 'merge_status': it is true when merge_status equals the string 'can_be_merged', false otherwise. The raw merge_status string is not directly emitted; only the derived boolean 'can_be_merged' is output. And for GitLab milestone, it uses 'due_date' and 'start_date' as wire keys (not 'due_on'). These are exposed as epoch millisecond timestamps in the output. The GitLab milestone also exposes 'project_id', 'iid', and 'web_url' fields not present in GitHub milestones.

One other implementation split to keep clean: the stdin/stdout adapter is solely responsible for reading a JSON command envelope, routing by 'view' key, calling into the core model layer, and formatting output. The core model and parsing layer must have no dependency on stdin/stdout or the JSON command envelope format. Diff statistics computation must depend on an abstraction for running a command, not a concrete process launcher. Also for the 'file_contents' view, the adapter outputs the value of the 'content' field verbatim, with no trailing newline appended beyond what is already in the content string itself. The output is exactly the raw string value of 'data.content'.

trigger_keywords: missing optional fields
null handling for absent keys
what happens when a field is not in the payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Absent optional attributes must decode to a neutral null marker and render as the literal string 'null' in the output. Unknown or extra keys in the wire payload must be silently ignored. The library must never throw or fail due to a missing optional field.

trigger_keywords: output format
what does the output look like
how are fields printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter outputs one 'key=value' line per field, terminated with a newline. Nested objects are flattened using dotted key paths (e.g. 'user.login=alice', 'head.repo.full_name=acme/widget'). List sizes are exposed as '*.count=N'. List elements are exposed with their zero-based index in the key path (e.g. 'assignees.0.login=bob'). The entire output ends with a trailing newline character.

trigger_keywords: platform detection logic
how to detect which platform
github vs gitlab vs bitbucket detection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Platform detection (view: 'platform_detection') inspects the top-level danger object in the payload for the presence of 'github', 'gitlab', or 'bitbucket' keys. It emits three boolean flags: 'is_github', 'is_gitlab', 'is_bitbucket' — exactly one of which is true. The detection is mutually exclusive.

trigger_keywords: changed lines zero commits
diff stats with no commits
what if commit_count is zero
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the 'changed_lines' view, if commit_count is 0 then additions, deletions, and lines_of_code must all be output as 0, regardless of any diff content present in the payload. Only when commit_count >= 1 should the diff string be parsed.

trigger_keywords: changed lines diff parsing
how to parse the diff string
numstat format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The diff string is in git numstat format: each line is 'additions TAB deletions TAB filepath'. The adapter sums additions across all lines for 'additions', sums deletions for 'deletions', and outputs their sum as 'lines_of_code'. Note: in the numstat format the first column is additions and the second is deletions.

trigger_keywords: file separation
project structure
how should code be organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be organized as multiple files/modules: per-platform model definitions (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket), shared value types (users, commits, timestamps, branch references), derived aggregations (diff statistics), and a separate I/O execution adapter. A single god-file is explicitly disallowed.

trigger_keywords: bitbucket server user fields
self-hosted user output
bitbucket user name vs login
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Bitbucket Server (self-hosted) user objects use 'name' (not 'login') as the primary identifier field. Output should use 'user.name=...' (not 'user.login'). Bitbucket Server users also have 'displayName', 'emailAddress', 'slug', 'active', and 'type' fields with their own camelCase wire names that must be mapped appropriately.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: danger kotlin view dispatcher
view field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter routes by the top-level 'view' field, not by 'action'. The 'data' field carries the platform payload; output is the flattened deterministic view requested by values such as github_issue, github_pull_request, gitlab_merge_request, bitbucket_pull_request, platform_detection, changed_files, changed_lines, and file_contents.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same idea as that separation we did on the login module integration
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Strict separation of concerns where the I/O execution adapter (stdin/stdout, JSON command routing) is a separate module from the core parsing/model layer — the adapter calls into the model, the model has no awareness of the adapter

context_pointers.vague_reference: timestamps should come out as numbers not strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ISO-8601 timestamp strings must be parsed and converted to epoch milliseconds (Long integer). Already-integer timestamps from Bitbucket Server pass through as-is. Absent timestamps render as the literal string 'null'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please refer to the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output contract, and follow the same patterns established by the other platform parsers already in the codebase.

feature10_bitbucket_commits.json.description: Parse the server commit list, exposing each commit's full and short SHA, author, author/committer epoch timestamps, message and parent SHAs.

feature11_bitbucket_comments.json.description: Parse the server comment activity stream, exposing each entry's id, epoch timestamp, author, action and (when present) the nested comment detail with its repository id.

feature12_bitbucket_metadata.json.description: Parse the server pull-request metadata into repository slug and pull-request id.

feature13_bitbucket_activities.json.description: Parse the server activity stream (OPENING/RESCOPING/etc), exposing each activity's id, epoch timestamp, actor account and action.

feature14_changed_files.json.description: Expose the modified, created and deleted file path lists from the git metadata block.

cases.expected_output:
modified.count=3
modified.0=.travis.yml
modified.1=Podfile
modified.2=Kiosk/App/Logger.swift
created.count=1
created.0=.ruby-version
deleted.count=1
deleted.0=Obsolete.swift

feature15_1_changed_lines.json.description: Aggregate a unified numstat diff into total additions, deletions and combined lines of code for a pull request that has at least one commit.

feature15_2_changed_lines_no_commits.jso.description: When the pull request has no commits there is no diff range, so additions, deletions and lines of code are all zero regardless of any diff content.

feature16_file_contents.json.description: Read the textual contents of a file referenced by path and return it verbatim.

feature1_github_pull_request.json.description: Parse a hosted Git platform pull-request payload and expose its scalar fields, timestamps as epoch milliseconds, nested author/assignee accounts, and head/base branch references.

feature6_platform_detection.json.description: Given a complete review payload, detect which hosting platform produced it and expose the three mutually-exclusive platform flags.
